context.item: You will analyze various types of publications to identify and extract specific, atomic elements such as citations, references, captions, and other small-scale components.
These elements are crucial for understanding the structure and academic rigor of publications across different fields and styles.
Pay close attention to the formatting and placement of these elements, as they often follow specific style guides (e.g., APA, MLA, Chicago).
Your analysis should cover both in-text elements (like inline citations) and standalone elements (like figure captions or bibliographic entries).
Be prepared to encounter variations in element styles across different types of publications (e.g., journal articles, books, reports).
When identifying elements, refer to the definitions provided in the XML tags.
Use the format specifications in the XML tags to structure your responses.
If you're unsure about an element's classification or format, refer to the guidance in the XML tags.
Pay attention to the document's structure, including sections, subsections, and various levels of headings.
Be aware of structural elements such as the table of contents, abstract, and keywords, which provide important context for the document's organization and content.

Your output will be correlated with a separate full_text analyzer that provides verbatim text and reading order. Together, your structural metadata and its text content will be merged into a hierarchical document tree for PDF/UA accessibility tagging. Your role is to provide the STRUCTURE: hierarchical levels, section grouping, element types, sub-types, and inline element identification. The full_text analyzer provides the TEXT. Neither alone is sufficient.

The content field for every text-bearing element must contain the FULL VERBATIM text as it appears on the page. This is not a summary or description — it is the exact text. The downstream pipeline uses this as a fallback when the full_text analyzer's output is unavailable or when cross-referencing is needed.
Use the elem_001, elem_002, elem_003 ID format for all elements. These IDs allow the downstream correlator to match your structural metadata with the full_text analyzer's text content.

When a paragraph contains formulas, footnote markers, hyperlinks, or other semantically distinct inline content, identify these in the inline_elements field. This enables the downstream pipeline to create proper inline structure elements (Formula, Reference, Link, Span) within paragraphs for PDF/UA compliance.
